nativeRange.habitat: Seasonal wetlands, marshes, and river margins in tropical East and Southeast Africa

decorPreferences: background placement with vertical room to grow
strong lighting to develop copper-bronze coloration
space stems apart so lower leaves receive adequate light

summary: Red Stem Nesaea (Nesaea crassicaulis) is a demanding background stem plant from tropical East and Southeast Africa, prized for its narrow leaves in copper-bronze to reddish-brown tones. It develops its best coloration only under strong light, CO2 injection, and consistent iron fertilization.
careNotes: Red Stem Nesaea is a stem plant native to tropical East and Southeast Africa, grown in the hobby for its bold reddish-brown to copper-bronze narrow leaves on thick reddish stems. It tends to be a less common find than its relative the Golden Nesaea ([fw-plant-133]). To bring out the characteristic copper-bronze to reddish-brown coloration, the plant generally requires high light (roughly 0.5–1 W/L, equivalent to around 30–50 PAR), pressurized CO2 (strongly recommended, though not strictly mandatory at lower stocking densities), and consistent dosing of iron and micronutrients alongside macrofertilizers. Under lower light, the plant tends to survive but remain a dull green and drop its lower leaves over time. A nutrient-rich substrate — aquasoil or an inert substrate supplemented with root tabs — is generally advised. The plant is sensitive to abrupt changes in water parameters, and sudden shifts can trigger melting, particularly with freshly planted tissue-culture stems that are still transitioning from above-water (emersed) to submerged (submersed) growth.
breedingNotes: Red Stem Nesaea is propagated by stem cuttings. Healthy side shoots or stem tops roughly 5–10 cm long and carrying several nodes are cut from a mature plant, the leaves stripped from the lower nodes, and the cutting replanted directly into a nutrient-rich substrate. Trimming the top of an established stem encourages the plant to push out bushier lateral growth lower down. Spacing stems apart when planting prevents the canopy from shading lower leaves and helps each stem remain compact and fully leafed. Propagation from seed is not practiced in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for family Lythraceae, scientific name Nesaea crassicaulis, adult height 6-12 in (15-30 cm shipped/young), pH 6.0-7.0, soft water preferred, moderate-to-high lighting, CO2 recommended, fast growth, background placement, medium care difficulty, stem-cutting propagation, nutrient-rich substrate, sensitivity to parameter swings, reddish-brown narrow-leaf coloration, and 'rarer sight in the hobby' note.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (eastern and southeastern Africa), adult size 30-50 cm at full submerged height, temperature 22-28 C, pH 6.0-8.0 (wider than Buce Plant), hardness 0-12 dGH, strong lighting requirement (0.5-1 W/L), CO2 highly recommended, fine gravel substrate, nutrient-rich water and root + liquid fertilization, stem-cutting propagation from side shoots and stem tops, medium-to-high difficulty.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Used to confirm taxonomy: family Lythraceae, order Myrtales, full scientific authority Nesaea crassicaulis (Guill. & Perr.) Koehne, and synonym Ammannia crassicaulis — supporting the Graham & Gandhi (2013) Nesaea-into-Ammannia revision noted across the genus.
